```lean
/-
  Template: Refactoring Lean 4 Modules
  =============================================
  REFERENCE DOCUMENT — not compiled.

  How to split, reorganise, and maintain Lean 4 modules so the project
  stays fast to build, easy to navigate, and friendly to git workflows.

  Audience : anyone adding or restructuring files in any Lean 4 project.

```

 ### §N  Title

```lean
`.
-/

-- import Mathlib.Tactic          -- commented out: reference file only

set_option autoImplicit false
```

 ### §1  When to Split a Module

  #### Size thresholds

  | Lines       | Action                                             |
  |-------------|----------------------------------------------------|
  | < 500       | Ideal for leaf modules (tactics, predicates)       |
  | 500 – 2 000 | Acceptable for any layer                           |
  | 2 000 – 2 500 | Consider splitting soon                          |
  | > 2 500     | MUST split into .Core + .Lemmas + .Main            |
  | > 3 000     | BAD — causes full recompilation on every edit      |

  #### Why large files are problematic

  1. **Hash-based invalidation.**
     Lean's build system hashes every `.lean` file.  ANY change to a file
     (even a comment!) invalidates the `.olean` for that file AND forces a
     recompile of every file that imports it, directly or transitively.
     A 3 000-line file that sits high in the import graph therefore causes
     massive rebuild cascades on trivial edits.

  2. **Memory pressure during elaboration.**
     The elaborator loads the entire file into memory at once.  Files above
     ~2 500 lines can push peak RSS past comfortable limits, especially when
     Mathlib typeclass search is involved.

  3. **Slow IDE feedback.**
     The language server re-elaborates from the top of the file on each
     keystroke.  Larger files mean longer latency before the yellow squiggly
     lines settle.

  4. **Poor git-merge behaviour.**
     Two developers editing different sections of the same large file will
     trigger merge conflicts even when the changes are logically independent.
     Splitting those sections into separate files eliminates the conflict
     entirely.

 ### §2  Splitting Patterns

  Choose the pattern that best fits the module's content.

  #### Pattern A — Core + Extensions  (this style)

  ```
  MyModule/
    Core.lean         -- Types, structures, basic lemmas     (< 1 500 lines)
    Extensions.lean   -- Advanced theorems, bridges          (< 1 500 lines)
  MyModule.lean       -- Hub: imports Core + Extensions, re-exports
  ```

  Use this when the module has a clear "foundational" layer and an "advanced"
  layer.  Most Lean modules follow this pattern today.

  #### Pattern B — Defs + Basic + Instances  (Mathlib style)

  ```
  MyModule/
    Defs.lean         -- Pure definitions, no proofs
    Basic.lean        -- Fundamental lemmas about those defs
    Instances.lean    -- Typeclass instances (Decidable, Fintype, …)
  MyModule.lean       -- Hub: imports all three, re-exports
  ```

  Mathlib uses this when typeclass instances are heavy enough to warrant
  isolation.  Good when you want downstream files to import Defs alone
  without pulling in instance search.

  #### Pattern C — Topic-based  (for very large modules)

  ```
  MyModule/
    Types.lean        -- Core types and constructors
    Arithmetic.lean   -- Arithmetic lemmas
    Topology.lean     -- Topological properties
    Bridge.lean       -- Cross-domain connections
  MyModule.lean       -- Hub
  ```

  Use when a single domain naturally decomposes into several independent
  sub-topics, each with enough content to fill 500+ lines.

  #### Choosing a pattern

  * Default to Pattern A.
  * Escalate to Pattern C only when Pattern A produces sub-files > 1 500 lines.
  * Pattern B is appropriate when you have many typeclass instances that
    downstream consumers may or may not need.

 ### §3  Step-by-Step Refactoring Workflow

  1. **Backup.**
     Commit your current state to git.  You want a clean diff to review
     once the refactoring is done, and a safe rollback point.

  2. **Create the subdirectory.**
     ```bash
     mkdir MyProject/MyModule
     ```

  3. **Create the hub file.**
     `MyProject/MyModule.lean` should contain only imports:
     ```lean
     import MyProject.MyModule.Core
     import MyProject.MyModule.Extensions
     ```
     No definitions, no proofs — just imports.

  4. **Move definitions.**
     Cut entire `section … end` blocks (or coherent groups of defs + lemmas)
     from the original file and paste them into Core.lean or Extensions.lean.
     Keep the original `namespace MyProject.MyModule` in both sub-files.

  5. **Fix imports.**
     Each sub-file must declare its own imports.  Core.lean should NOT import
     Extensions.lean; Extensions.lean MAY import Core.lean.

  6. **Verify.**
     ```bash
     lake build MyProject.MyModule
     ```
     Must succeed with zero errors.

  7. **Update consumers.**
     Any file that previously did `import MyProject.MyModule` should still
     compile unchanged, because the hub re-exports everything.  Run:
     ```bash
     lake build
     ```

  8. **Clean unused imports.**
     ```bash
     lake exe shake
     ```
     Remove any imports that `shake` flags as unused.

 ### §4  Hub File Pattern (Re-Export)

  The hub file is the public API of a split module.  It imports the
  sub-modules and nothing else.  Consumers import the hub, never the
  sub-files directly (unless they deliberately want a narrow dependency).

  ```lean
  -- File: MyProject/MyModule.lean
  import MyProject.MyModule.Core
  import MyProject.MyModule.Extensions
  -- No definitions, no proofs — just imports.
  -- Consumers write `import MyProject.MyModule` and get everything.
  ```

  Because Lean re-exports all imported names by default, consumers see
  every definition from Core and Extensions through the hub, at the
  same fully-qualified path they had before the split.

 ### §5  Moving Definitions Safely

  **Golden rule:** move the definition AND every lemma that mentions it.

  * If a lemma in Core.lean references a definition in Extensions.lean,
    the split is wrong — you have a backward dependency.  Move the lemma
    down to Extensions, or move the definition up to Core.

  * Dependencies must flow in one direction only:
    ```
    Core  →  Extensions     (Extensions may import Core)
    Core  ←✗  Extensions    (Core must NEVER import Extensions)
    ```

  * After moving, use `#check` at the bottom of each sub-file to verify
    that key names resolve:
    ```lean
    #check MyProject.MyModule.myMainDef
    #check MyProject.MyModule.myMainLemma
    ```

  * Use the `export` command in the hub if you want names to be available
    without the sub-namespace prefix:
    ```lean
    export MyProject.MyModule.Core (myMainDef myHelper)
    ```

 ### §6  DAG Layer Discipline

  Every module belongs to exactly one layer.  The fundamental rule:

  ```
  Layer N may only import Layer ≤ N − 1.
  ```

  Violating this rule creates import cycles or gratuitous rebuild chains.

  #### Layer definitions

  | Layer | Role                    | Rule                             |
  |-------|-------------------------|----------------------------------|
  | 0     | Foundation              | NO analysis imports              |
  | 1     | Core theory             | Imports Layer 0 only             |
  | 2     | Advanced theory         | Imports Layer 0 – 1              |
  | 3     | Integration / umbrella  | Imports Layer 0 – 2              |
  | 4     | Index                   | Imports everything, defines nothing |

  #### Example DAG assignment

  ```
  Layer 0 (leaf):  Tactics   QualityGates   PhaseClassification   Profiling

  Layer 1:         CuspCatastrophe   CCVGating   EvaluationGaps

  Layer 2:         PipelineAdaptive      PhasePortrait
                   StochasticCCV         ProvenanceChain
                   ReinforcementLearning AgenticSafety
                   PipelineBypass        FeedbackSemantics

  Layer 3 (top):   LyapunovStability     CCVEContraction
  ```

  If you need to add a new module, decide its layer BEFORE writing code.
  A file that imports both Layer 0 and Layer 2 modules belongs to Layer 3.

 ### §7  Import Organisation

  #### Ordering convention

  1. **Mathlib imports first**, sorted alphabetically.
  2. **Project imports second**, ordered by layer (leaf → application).
  3. Blank line between Mathlib block and project block.

  ```lean
  import Mathlib.Analysis.NormedSpace.Basic
  import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Defs
  import Mathlib.Topology.MetricSpace.Basic

  import MyProject.Tactics
  import MyProject.Foundations
  import MyProject.Catastrophe.Core
  ```

  #### Narrow imports

  Prefer narrow, specific imports over broad ones:
  ```lean
  -- GOOD: pulls in only what you need
  import Mathlib.Data.Nat.Defs

  -- BAD: pulls in the entire tactic library (~200 files)
  import Mathlib.Tactic
  ```

  **Exception:** automation-heavy modules (Tactics.lean itself, or files
  whose primary purpose is tactic-based proofs) may import `Mathlib.Tactic`
  because they genuinely need most of it.

  #### Guarding layer boundaries

  Use `assert_not_exists` to enforce that a definition from a higher layer
  has NOT been imported:
  ```lean
  -- In a Layer 0 file: prove we haven't pulled in Layer 1 types
  assert_not_exists MyProject.Catastrophe.CuspState
  ```

  #### Finding unused imports

  ```bash
  lake exe shake        -- available in toolchain ≥ v4.28
  ```
  Remove every import that `shake` reports as unused.

 ### §8  Namespace Management for Refactored Modules

  After splitting, keep the **same** `namespace` in every sub-file so that
  names resolve at the same path they had before.

  ```lean
  -- In MyProject/MyModule/Core.lean
  namespace MyProject.MyModule

  def myDef : Nat := 42

  end MyProject.MyModule
  ```

  ```lean
  -- In MyProject/MyModule/Extensions.lean
  namespace MyProject.MyModule

  theorem myLemma : myDef = 42 := rfl

  end MyProject.MyModule
  ```

  Consumers still write `MyProject.MyModule.myDef` — the split is invisible.

  #### Visibility modifiers

  * `protected` — use for names that might conflict with Mathlib or other
    Lean modules (e.g., `protected def norm`).
  * `private` — use for truly internal helpers that no consumer should ever
    reference.  Private names are erased from the .olean.
  * `export` — use in the hub to lift sub-namespace names one level up.

 ### §9  Build Cache and Performance Impact

  #### Multiple `lean_lib` targets

  The lakefile can declare separate `lean_lib` entries for module subsets.
  Each target has its own `.olean` cache directory, so changes in one
  target do not invalidate caches of another.

  ```lean
  -- In lakefile.lean
  lean_lib MyProjectFoundation where
    srcDir := "MyProject"
    roots := #[`MyProject.Tactics, `MyProject.Foundations, `MyProject.Profiling]

  lean_lib MyProject where
    srcDir := "MyProject"
    roots := #[`MyProject]        -- catches everything else
  ```

  #### When to add a new `lean_lib` target

  * The module set changes independently from the rest of the project
    (e.g., foundation tactics rarely change once stable).
  * The module set is imported by many consumers — isolating it avoids
    cascading rebuilds when unrelated modules change.

  #### Practical effect

  Splitting `StochasticCCV/Core.lean` (3 724 lines) into three files means
  an edit to the MetricSpace section no longer forces recompilation of the
  Doeblin section or its downstream consumers.

 ### §10  Files Likely To Need Refactoring

  Based on line-count analysis:

  | File                              | Lines | Recommended Action                        |
  |-----------------------------------|-------|-------------------------------------------|
  | StochasticCCV/Core.lean           | 3 724 | Split: extract MetricSpace + Doeblin      |
  | ProvenanceChain/Extensions.lean   | 3 246 | Split: 10 independent sections → files    |
  | LyapunovStability.lean            | 3 072 | Split: extract LTS bridge (line 2 631+)   |

  All three exceed the 3 000-line "BAD" threshold and are causing
  unnecessary rebuild cascades today.

  Priority order:
  1. StochasticCCV/Core — highest line count, most importers.
  2. ProvenanceChain/Extensions — cleanest split (independent sections).
  3. LyapunovStability — requires careful dependency analysis first.

 ### §11  Redundant Import Cleanup

  A common redundancy in some codebases:

  ```lean
  import Mathlib.Tactic            -- provides all Mathlib tactics
  import MyProject.Tactics             -- ALSO imports Mathlib.Tactic internally
  ```

  The first line is redundant because `MyProject.Tactics` already re-exports
  everything from `Mathlib.Tactic`.  Removing it saves a few hundred
  milliseconds of import resolution and makes the dependency graph cleaner.

  Currently 12 files in your project carry this redundancy.  To find them:

  ```bash
  grep -l 'import Mathlib.Tactic' <lean-dir>/**/*.lean | \
    xargs grep -l 'import MyProject.Tactics'
  ```

  For each file found, remove the `import Mathlib.Tactic` line and verify
  with `lake build`.

 ### §12  Refactoring Checklist

  Before merging a refactoring PR, verify every item:

  - [ ] All files < 2 500 lines
  - [ ] DAG layers correctly assigned (see §6)
  - [ ] No import cycles (`lake build` catches these)
  - [ ] `assert_not_exists` guards on layer boundaries
  - [ ] Hub files contain ONLY imports (no defs, no proofs)
  - [ ] `lake build` succeeds with 0 errors, 0 warnings
  - [ ] `lake exe shake` reports no unused imports
  - [ ] Consumer imports unchanged (hub re-exports everything)
  - [ ] `#print axioms` unchanged for key theorems
        (splitting must not introduce new axioms)
  - [ ] Git diff reviewed for accidental deletions
        (`git diff --stat` should show zero net line loss)

  A refactoring PR should change ZERO proofs.  If a proof needs editing
  to accommodate the split, that is a sign the dependency direction is
  wrong — revisit the split boundary.
